body:
### Describe your issue

## Background

While investigating a compatibility breakage between SciPy nightlies and scikit-learn ([scikit-learn#33616](https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/33616), a recurrence of [scipy#18377](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/18377)), I found it necessary to read the Cython compiler source in detail to understand exactly what constitutes a binary-incompatible change to a `.pxd` file. That information does not appear to be documented anywhere. This issue proposes adding it.

## What actually happens, and where in the source

The Cython documentation describes `.pxd` files as working "like C header files". This is not actually the case when it comes to binary compatibility of `cimport`'ing across packages.

There are **two separate checking mechanisms**, operating at different times and with different rules.

### 1. Compile-time check: `.pxd` vs `.pyx` declaration mismatch

When a `.pxd` and a `.pyx` file share the same name, Cython merges them during compilation. It calls `same_c_signature_as_resolved_type` (and relatives `compatible_signature_with_resolved_type`, `narrower_c_signature_than_resolved_type`) in [`Cython/Compiler/PyrexTypes.py`](https://github.com/cython/cython/blob/master/Cython/Compiler/PyrexTypes.py) to decide whether the declarations are compatible. Crucially, type comparison at this stage calls `resolve()` on both sides, which **strips typedefs** down to the underlying primitive - so `ctypedef int blas_int` and `int` are considered the same type at compile time, and no error is raised.

The merge and error reporting happens in [`Symtab.ModuleScope.declare_cfunction`](https://github.com/cython/cython/blob/master/Cython/Compiler/Symtab.py), with the compile-time error `"Function signature does not match previous declaration"` emitted there.

### 2. Runtime check: `cimport` across pre-compiled modules

This is the check that causes the `TypeError: C function X has wrong signature` error
at import time. The mechanism lives in [`Cython/Utility/ImportExport.c`](https://github.com/cython/cython/blob/master/Cython/Utility/ImportExport.c):

- The **exporting** module (e.g. `scipy.linalg.cython_blas`) calls  `__Pyx_ExportFunction` during its `PyInit_*`, which stores each exported function as a `PyCapsule` in a module-level dict called `__pyx_capi__`. The capsule's **name**  is the function's C type signature, rendered as a string by   `CFuncType.signature_string()` → `empty_declaration_code()` → `declaration_code("")`  in `PyrexTypes.py`.

- The **importing** module calls `__Pyx_ImportFunction_<cyversion>` → `__Pyx_ImportFromPxd_<cyversion>`, which retrieves the capsule from `__pyx_capi__`  and calls `PyCapsule_IsValid(cobj, expected_sig)`. This is a **plain C string comparison** of the expected signature (baked in at the importer's compile time) against the stored capsule name. There is no typedef resolution, no semantic equivalence - only byte-for-byte string equality.

The practical consequence: a `ctypedef int blas_int` introduced in a `.pxd` changes the capsule string for every function that uses `blas_int` as a parameter type, from e.g. `"double (int *, double *, int *)"` to `"double (__pyx_t_5scipy_6linalg_11cython_blas_blas_int *, double *, __pyx_t_5scipy_6linalg_11cython_blas_blas_int *)"`, breaking all previously compiled cimporters - even though the underlying C type is identical. The mangled name `__pyx_t_5scipy_6linalg_11cython_blas_blas_int` encodes the full dotted module path of the `.pxd` that introduced the typedef, using a length-prefixed scheme (`5scipy_`, `6linalg_`, `11cython_blas_`).

The same asymmetry applies to `const`: `CConstOrVolatileType.same_as_resolved_type` accepts `const T` where `T` was declared (so the compile-time check silently passes), but `declaration_code` emits `"const "` into the capsule string, so the runtime check fails.

## Documentation gap

The [`.pxd` files tutorial page](https://cython.readthedocs.io/en/latest/src/tutorial/pxd_files.html) says only that `.pxd` files "work like C header files", which is misleading. The [sharing declarations page](https://cython.readthedocs.io/en/latest/src/userguide/sharing_declarations.html) describes the mechanism superficially but gives no guidance on what changes are binary-safe and what are not. Neither page mentions the `__pyx_capi__` dict, `PyCapsule`, or the possibility of runtime `TypeError` from signature mismatches.

Since `.pxd` files are the primary ABI surface for any compiled Cython library that is cimported by third parties, this gap regularly causes real-world breakage (the scipy/scikit-learn breakage has now occurred twice).

## Proposed documentation additions

The following two tables summarize what I believe to be the complete rules, derived from reading source code (with help of Claude, as declared further down) and validation of some of these rules with SciPy builds.

### Complete binary interface of a cimported module

| Interface element | Mechanism | Changes that break it | Failure |
|---|---|---|---|
| `cdef` function signature | `PyCapsule` in `__pyx_capi__`, name = C type declaration string | Any change to argument or return types (including `const`, calling convention, typedef introduction/removal); switching `cdef` ↔ `cpdef`; adding optional args; moving the `.pxd` to a different package path | `TypeError` at import time |
| `cdef` variable type | `PyCapsule` in `__pyx_capi__`, name = C type declaration string | Same as above | `TypeError` at import time |
| `cdef class` struct size | `tp_basicsize` comparison in `__Pyx_ImportType_<cyversion>` | Adding, removing, or changing `cdef` attributes | `ValueError` at import time |
| `cdef class` vtable layout | Raw `void*` capsule with `name=NULL`, **no validation** | Adding, removing, or reordering `cdef` methods | Silent wrong-function-pointer call, undefined behaviour |
| `cdef inline` functions | Textually inlined into each TU; no runtime check | Signature changes caught at compile time only | Compile-time error |
| `cdef extern from` declarations | Not exported at all | N/A | Linker error or silent type mismatch |
| Fused-type specializations | One capsule entry per concrete type, key = `__pyx_fuse_N` + name | Reordering types within a `ctypedef fused` block changes which specialization is at index N | `TypeError` at import time |

### Capsule signature string for common types

The capsule string is the output of `declaration_code("")` (non-pyrex, non-display path) on each argument and return type:

| Cython type | Capsule string |
|---|---|
| `int` | `int` |
| `unsigned int` | `unsigned int` |
| `long long` | `PY_LONG_LONG` (not `long long` — a Cython-internal macro name) |
| `unsigned long long` | `unsigned PY_LONG_LONG` |
| `double` | `double` |
| `bint` | `int` |
| `char *` | `char *` |
| `const double *` | `const double *` |
| `ctypedef int foo` (private, top-level in `.pxd`) | `__pyx_t_<length-prefixed-module-path>_foo` |
| `ctypedef int foo` (inside `cdef extern from`) | `foo` |
| `int[10]` as argument | `int *` (array parameters decay to pointers, as in C) |
| `object`, `list`, `dict`, … | `PyObject *` (all Python object types are identical in the capsule) |
| `MyExtClass` (cdef class used as argument) | `struct __pyx_obj_<module>_MyExtClass *` |
| `double[:]`, `double[::1]`, `double[:, :]` (any memoryview) | `__Pyx_memviewslice` (layout is erased) |
| `const double[:]` | `__Pyx_memviewslice` (const on memoryview dtype does **not** appear) |
| `cpdef` hidden dispatch argument | `int` (the invisible `__pyx_skip_dispatch` arg) |
| No arguments | `void` |

Note that `long long` appearing as `PY_LONG_LONG` in capsule strings means Cython's built-in `long long` type and a `ctypedef long long foo` (extern) type named `foo` produce *different* capsule strings even on a platform where they are the same C type - another source of unexpected incompatibility.

## How to inspect a module's current capsule strings

```python
import ctypes, scipy.linalg.cython_blas as mod

_get_name = ctypes.pythonapi.PyCapsule_GetName
_get_name.restype = ctypes.c_char_p
_get_name.argtypes = [ctypes.py_object]

for name, capsule in sorted(mod.__pyx_capi__.items()):
    print(f"{name}: {_get_name(capsule).decode()}")
```

In https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/24938 I added a script to generate regression tests for ABI stability. Unless these string comparisons can be improved, it looks like any package that releases a Cython API with wheels (or anywhere where ABI stability is assumed) should be adding a test along those lines.

## AI usage disclosure

This research was carried out with substantial assistance from Claude Sonnet, in particular to dig into the Cython internals of how the compile-time and runtime checks work in detail. I spent most of today digging into this problem to fix the scipy/sklearn incompatibility issue, so I'm fairly sure that the content in this issue is largely correct. It may of course be incomplete, inaccurate or suboptimal - this is nontrivial to understand.

comment_data.body:
We've definitely had requests to do something about `const` creating a different output before. I haven't done it mostly because I haven't come up with a good solution. Most changes we do now end up being hard breaks.

In the fairly recent past we've resisted adding documentation for `__pyx_capi__ ` (on the basis that it's an internal implementation detail). But clearly "what matches" is somewhat public.

If we wanted to improve it, the remaining place we have available to store ABI metadata is the `context` pointer of the capsule. If used, that should be used in a way that can be extended because there isn't a further place to add more data.  One possibility would be to be able to manually specify `@cython.compatible_signature` on a function to manually add a list of signatures that also match (i.e. to manually work around adding `const` or changing to a typedef). That's very manual but it would likely be fairly simple to implement and let users work around the most common problems.

> In the fairly recent past we've resisted adding documentation for `__pyx_capi__ ` (on the basis that it's an internal implementation detail). But clearly "what matches" is somewhat public.

How about as a stopgap, something like a simple `.. warning::` on https://cython.readthedocs.io/en/latest/src/tutorial/pxd_files.html that links to this issue? That doesn't commit you to documenting `__pyx_capi__` but at least warns users that they have to inspect these signatures (and clean them up) before committing to any public API/ABI.



> One possibility would be to be able to manually specify `@cython.compatible_signature` on a function to manually add a list of signatures that also match

Something like that would really help I think. It's not just signatures though, it may also be the keys of the `__pyx_capi__` dict. Example:
```
>>> import ctypes, scipy.special.cython_special as mod
... 
... _get_name = ctypes.pythonapi.PyCapsule_GetName
... _get_name.restype = ctypes.c_char_p
... _get_name.argtypes = [ctypes.py_object]
... 
... for name, capsule in sorted(mod.__pyx_capi__.items()):
...     print(f"{name}: {_get_name(capsule).decode()}")

<snip>
__pyx_fuse_2smirnovi: double (Py_ssize_t, double, int __pyx_skip_dispatch)
__pyx_fuse_2yn: double (Py_ssize_t, double, int __pyx_skip_dispatch)
agm: double (double, double, int __pyx_skip_dispatch)
bei: double (double, int __pyx_skip_dispatch)
```

So half the keys end up in there with their plain function name, and half with a mangled `__pyx_fuse_xxx` name. Even a basic refactoring, addition (say trying to add a second version of a function for an additional type), or removal of a second type will be breaking. 

I think to improve this, you'd want a remapping from old name/signature to new name/signature. We already have a concrete case of only the name changing that looks like it needs fixing before the next SciPy release: https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/24937

I agree with regard to Cython ABI both the Cython language guarantee and its docs have been super unclear. Previously, I've asked if we could at least document `__pyx_capi__` (https://github.com/cython/cython/issues/3077, https://github.com/cython/cython/issues/6484, https://github.com/cython/cython/pull/6487) but it did not go anywhere. 

@mdboom from our team has been investigating for quite some time the scope of "Cython ABI" (it is a muddy concept unclear to our C++-expert colleagues) and how to detect potential breakage (https://github.com/NVIDIA/cuda-python/pull/914#issuecomment-3229785966, https://github.com/NVIDIA/cuda-python/issues/1030, https://github.com/NVIDIA/cuda-python/pull/1067, https://github.com/NVIDIA/cuda-python/issues/1663), but we did not land on any concrete solution either. It would be really nice if we could figure something out through this discussion 🙂

> Previously, I've asked if we could at least document `__pyx_capi__` ([#3077](https://github.com/cython/cython/issues/3077), [#6484](https://github.com/cython/cython/issues/6484), [#6487](https://github.com/cython/cython/pull/6487)) but it did not go anywhere.

That looks like the right intent, but the wrong ask (it's about the semi-private implementation detail itself rather than the observable effects of it). Focusing on `__pyx_capi__` seems counterproductive - the real ask is to document (a) the semantic guarantees of ABI stability when touching `.pxd` files, and (b) how to test that. The former is about features. And I think the latter can also be done without even touching `__pyx_capi__`: in the end you can achieve exactly the same thing by just reading the `.pxd` file itself. The requirement seems to be that you can insert signatures on new lines into a `.pxd`, and you can reorder existing lines, but you cannot change existing signatures in any way whatsoever. Nor move the `.pxd` around in the installed tree.

The above tables I posted are about the more granular effects of that. E.g., if you have a `double` routine and want to add a `float` version of it, the natural way would be to change it to a fused type `float_t` that maps to double/float. Which is breaking in the `.pxd` file. Simply adding a new `float` signature (even if the `.pyx` does use fused types) is fine.

> But clearly "what matches" is somewhat public.

Surely that is not only somewhat public, it's 100% public. Which is why Numba, SciPy, cuda-python, etc. all keep shooting themselves in the foot with it. Let's document that?

title: Document the binary interface of cimported modules (`.pxd` ABI rules, capsule signature checks)
